questions.question: Why is origin protection a first-class concern in an edge gateway?
options: Because the origin is often the most fragile part of the system and retries or regional shifts can amplify failure there.
Because edge gateways are never allowed to terminate TLS directly.
Because auth always runs only at the origin tier.
Because POP routing eliminates all latency concerns automatically.
questions.explanation: An edge gateway can either protect origins or accidentally overwhelm them during partial failures.

questions.question: What is a retry budget intended to prevent?
options: Any form of regional failover during incidents.
The use of cacheable endpoints at the edge.
Unlimited retries that turn partial origin slowness into a broader outage.
TLS certificate rotation across POPs.
questions.explanation: Retry budgets cap amplification so retries remain a tool, not a failure multiplier.

questions.question: Why is policy version visibility important across POPs?
options: It helps detect rollout drift so different POPs do not enforce different auth or routing behavior unknowingly.
It guarantees that all requests become cacheable.
It removes the need for region-level monitoring.
It makes origin shielding optional.
questions.explanation: Version skew is a real operational risk when policy is pushed globally.

questions.question: Which auth strategy usually gives the best balance for an edge gateway?
options: A hybrid approach that keeps some decisions local while still allowing centralized policy checks where needed.
Always forwarding every auth decision to a single global service.
Disabling auth at the edge and trusting only the origin.
Failing open for every route whenever auth gets slower than expected.
questions.explanation: Hybrid designs often balance latency, freshness, and dependency risk.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of an origin shield layer?
options: To increase origin request fanout across more regions.
To centralize and smooth requests before they hit origins, reducing blast radius and improving locality.
To replace all edge caches with a single global cache.
To make retry budgets unnecessary.
questions.explanation: Shielding can absorb bursts and reduce the number of directly exposed origin requests.

questions.question: Which observability signal is most useful during a partial origin slowness incident?
options: Only total request count across the whole fleet.
Latency and retry metrics broken down by POP, region, route, and origin pool.
The number of architecture diagrams stored in the runbook.
Certificate expiration time for unrelated hostnames.
questions.explanation: Regional and origin-pool granularity is critical when the failure is partial rather than global.

questions.question: What trade-off does local token validation at the edge introduce?
options: Lower latency and fewer dependency hops, but potentially more policy freshness complexity.
Perfect global consistency at the cost of higher storage use.
No need for any TLS or routing configuration.
Guaranteed origin cache hits for dynamic traffic.
questions.explanation: Local validation is fast, but freshness and revocation semantics need deliberate handling.

questions.question: If one region is slow but not down, what is the strongest first design concern?
options: Making sure retries and failover do not overload other regions or the same weak origin pool.
Disabling all logging to reduce CPU usage.
Increasing TLS handshake size to slow clients evenly.
Removing rate limiting so users can retry more often.
questions.explanation: Partial failures often become major incidents because the mitigation path amplifies them.
